blocks.desc: Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L20 split (1 of 2). What labor cost is in a professional kitchen, why it often exceeds ingredient cost. Estimate prep time with the chef's input. Conceptual + estimation only — calculation lives in the next block.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate

L3. Digestive chemistry: amylase on starch, stomach acid on protein, bile on fat, absorption across the small intestine wall. Demonstrate the iodine test live so the chemistry is concrete. 5/6 stays at the demo level; 7/8 engages with enzyme/bond-breaking mechanism and surface area.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 2 on calories as limited metric
blocks.desc: L36. Seminar on a short reading about why calories alone is a poor metric for nutrition. Same format as Reading 1. 5/6 gets a simpler/adapted version of both readings.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Decimal fluency: addition/subtraction on ingredient prices
blocks.desc: Decimal addition/subtraction on real ingredient prices. Fluency block — students need this solid before retail-pricing calculations land. Pull dollar figures from the actual grocery shopping list.

G8 introduction of the cost-function form C = pn + b (8.F.B.4), where p = price per ingredient unit, n = quantity, b = fixed costs. Direct instruction with worked examples on one ingredient: price-per-pound times pounds-needed gives the variable cost; fixed costs (overhead, packaging) add the b. This block introduces the machinery that the wholesale/retail/labor lessons in wks 5-6 will populate. Sets up tomorrow's plotting work and the wk6 cost-model assembly.

G7/G8 kitchen visit debrief + revised cost model (7.RP.A.3). Lesson 39 from the Challenge spec. 60 minutes — the longer block reflects that students need time to compare actual prep time observed in the partner kitchen against their wk5 estimates and propagate any change through the cost model. The revision is non-trivial: a longer-than-expected prep time can push a day over the $10 ceiling, which forces the team back to either menu adjustments or a margin discussion with the chef before the panel.
L29 split (2 of 2). Role-play the meeting. Every student introduces themselves and asks one of their brief questions out loud before they ever do it for real. Guides play the chef.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L18 (G5). Retail vs wholesale: why the gap exists, what volume buying does to unit cost. G5 calculates the dollar difference for their own ingredient list. Comes after the wholesale supplier visit so they have real numbers to work with.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L41. Edit kitchen-visit footage into a 60–90 second video for the partner's social media. Content serves the restaurant, not the student. Clean cuts and audible audio first; transitions and color come if there's time.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate

L29 split (1 of 2). What a professional client meeting looks like. Introductions, what you need to learn before you leave, what to do when you don't understand the chef, how to take useful notes during the meeting.

L13 split (2 of 2). Unit conversion within weight — ounces ↔ pounds. Real recipe quantities. 5/6 must be fluent here before any scaling happens. 7/8 again moves toward cross-system in a later session.
L1. Pre-instruction math diagnostic. NJSLS prerequisites for the upcoming challenge math. Not graded — flags students needing closer monitoring.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L43 + L44 combined. Presentation structure (sections, owners, evidence flow, what panel will ask) followed by team draft assembly in the same block. Section assignments are guide-decided — no student self-selects. Younger students must own a substantive section.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

G7 fluency warmup on rational-number operations (7.NS.A.3) before scaling and cost work begins. Mixed-number multiplication, signed-decimal arithmetic, fraction-of-quantity questions framed in recipe and dollar contexts. Pure procedural fluency — no application yet. Diagnostic-informed: students flagged for slow recall on the Day-1 diagnostic get direct instruction; the rest run application items. Goal: rational-number operations are automatic before they get embedded in two- and three-step problems next week.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 1 on food and the body
blocks.desc: L36. Seminar on a short reading about food and the body. Reading assigned the day before with 2–3 questions due on arrival. 15 min discussion max. Reinforces what's already been taught — does not introduce new content.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L5 follow-up. Insulin and metabolic regulation: cellular uptake of glucose, why the post-spike overshoot happens, why diet is a legitimate cognitive-performance variable, not just a health one. Pair with cognitive data once it's accumulating.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L30. Restaurant meeting debrief. What did you learn, what surprised you, what do you still need to find out, what math do you need to do now you have the actual recipe card. This lesson surfaces gaps before scaling work begins.

G7 practice on unit rate with fractions (7.RP.A.1). Mixed-difficulty problem set: computing rates, comparing two rates, finding total quantity from rate and count. Quantities pulled from real recipe formats so the procedural work happens in a context that previews the wk4 recipe scaling. Students who finished the concept block solid run the application problems; students who struggled get a short re-teach and then easier items.

L33 (cooked). Same tests on cooked samples — what changes when an ingredient is cooked? Compare to the raw results from earlier in the challenge. Strengthens the team's claim about what the menu actually delivers nutritionally.

L29 split (1 of 2). What a professional client meeting looks like. Introductions, what you need to learn before you leave, what to do when you don't understand the chef, how to take useful notes during the meeting.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L12. Direct instruction on multiplying a fraction by a whole number — the operation behind every recipe scaling task. 5/6: split the room based on the diagnostic; students who are fluent move straight to application. 7/8: students fluent on this start mixed numbers and improper fractions in the same session.

L2. The digestive system as a mechanical process: how food moves from ingestion through stomach to small intestine. 5/6: concrete language, factory-line analogy. 7/8: introduce peristalsis, bolus, chyme.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L9 split (1 of 2). USDA FoodData Central: how to navigate the database, find a whole food vs a packaged item, locate specific nutritional fields. Pure mechanics — students should be able to search and pull up a record fluently before interpretation comes in.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Chem tests: demo + practice (iodine / Benedict's / brown paper / Biuret)
blocks.desc: L7 + L8 combined into one 90-minute block (formerly 4 separate demo + practice sessions). Guide demo first on contrasting samples (whole foods vs processed) so the chemistry is concrete: iodine for starch (blue-black indicates starch), Benedict's for simple sugars (blue → orange → brick red with rising concentration when heated), brown paper spot test for fats (translucency under light), Biuret for protein (blue → purple). Students then run all four tests on a new sample set, recording results and writing interpretive statements. 5/6: simple statements ('starch present; can't tell how much'). 7/8: note degree of color change and what that suggests about relative concentration. Name the limit explicitly at the end: tests confirm presence and rough quantity, not grams. By end of session every student should be able to run all four tests independently.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L2. The digestive system as a mechanical process: how food moves from ingestion through stomach to small intestine. 5/6: concrete language, factory-line analogy. 7/8: introduce peristalsis, bolus, chyme.

Sits between gap-analysis (10:25 end) and chef communication (11:00 start) on wk7 d3. Math block surfaced day-nutrient gaps; this block translates each gap into a physiological-consequence sentence the chef pitch will use. 0-5 recap: gap analysis surfaced ~5 day-nutrient gaps; pick top 3. 5-15 modeling: take one gap (e.g., 'Tuesday is 12g short on protein') → write physiological consequence ('slower amino-acid pool refill → weaker repair signal post-lunch'). 15-30 team work: 1-sentence physiological-consequence statement for each of their 3 gaps. 30-35 stress-test: pick strongest sentence to feed into chef communication block. New context for MS-LS1-7 — applies cellular respiration knowledge to their own data; output is load-bearing for chef block.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

blocks.ttl: Fraction mult applied to recipe (I do)
blocks.desc: L12 'I do' for G5. Guide models scaling one full ingredient line from recipe-card unit to 30 servings on the board. Students follow only — independent work comes in the next block.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

blocks.ttl: Bar chart construction: axes and labeling
blocks.desc: L24 instructional half. Build the bar chart that becomes the team's visual: what goes on each axis, how to label, what makes a chart legible vs cluttered. Then apply to one dataset (cost or nutrition) in the same block.

External event. Each team meets their restaurant partner in person. Present the brief, hear chef's dish proposals, ask the three prepared questions, take notes. May span two days depending on partner availability.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Revise the brief based on guide review. Brief must be clean, specific, and answer-ready before the restaurant meeting. No team meets a partner with an unreviewed brief.

blocks.ttl: Client brief: model + team draft (I do / We do)
blocks.desc: L27 + L28 combined. Each team drafts the brief for their actual restaurant partner: $10/meal ceiling, nutritional targets, dietary constraints, serving size 30, three specific questions for the chef. Drafts get reviewed before the meeting — this block produces the draft, not the final.
Cost model finalization. Lock the draft. After kitchen visit, actual prep time replaces estimated and labor figures get revised. Final number defended at panel.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L9 split (2 of 2). What each nutritional field means and how to read it. Students look up three foods they actually eat and write a paragraph each on what the data says. Bring results to the next session.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.

L40 + L42 combined. Study the restaurant partner's existing online voice (Instagram, website). Write a brief style description. Then write captions for the team's static images and video in that voice. Caption must sound like the restaurant wrote it, not a student.

L6. Gut-brain axis and specific nutrient–system connections: serotonin in the gut, iron and oxygen transport, omega-3s and adolescent brain dev, protein → neurotransmitters. Why 'calories' obscures all of this. 5/6: the connections they can feel (nervous → stomach). 7/8: name the neurotransmitters and their precursors.

G7 labor-cost calculation as proportional reasoning (7.RP.A.2). Lesson 20 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 framing. Students take prep-time-in-hours from their chef (estimated during the wk4 Restaurant meeting), apply an hourly rate, and divide by 30 servings to get labor-per-meal. The proportional move: 'hours per meal' and 'dollars per meal' are both unit rates derived from the same prep-time figure. G7 students discuss with their chef whether labor scales linearly to 60 servings.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Added (5/6). Mean/median/mode applied to the actual cognitive data the class has been gathering. Pulls the statistics out of the abstract and into something they care about (their own scores).
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Criterion-based fluency gate covering all procedures from Weeks 2 through 6. 11 items, paper and pencil, no calculator, single sitting, 25 minutes testing plus 10 minutes guide scoring.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L15 + L16 combined. Scale the full recipe to 30 servings AND produce the shopping list from the scaled card in one continuous block. 5/6: same-system quantities only — guides pre-convert metric. 7/8: full conversions (incl. cross-system) by students. Output: a priced-ready shopping list for the grocery store visit.

L1. Pre-instruction math diagnostic for G7. NJSLS 6th-grade prerequisites: unit rate (6.RP.A.2) used in cost-per-meal and cost-per-ounce work, and multi-digit decimal computation (6.NS.B.3) used throughout the cost model. Not graded — flags students needing closer monitoring.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L3. Digestive chemistry: amylase on starch, stomach acid on protein, bile on fat, absorption across the small intestine wall. Demonstrate the iodine test live so the chemistry is concrete. 5/6 stays at the demo level; 7/8 engages with enzyme/bond-breaking mechanism and surface area.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

G8 plotting of C = pn + b on the coordinate plane (8.EE.B.5). Students take the function form they introduced yesterday and graph it for one ingredient — slope = price per unit, y-intercept = fixed cost. Build by hand on graph paper first, then check on Desmos. The visual move is: 'slope literally is the unit rate.' Sets up tomorrow's two-line graph (wholesale vs retail) where slope comparison becomes the primary analytic tool.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

Added (Week 6). How to make a professional call: how to introduce yourself, how to ask about pricing, how to record what you learn, how to handle a 'we'll call you back'. Practice on each other before any real supplier call.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

G7/G8 draft one-page Cost Model + P&L (7.RP.A.3). Lesson 23 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 only. Students compress yesterday's full cost model assembly into a single page: revenue line ($10/student × 30 servings × 5 days), cost line (ingredients + labor + overhead, totaled), margin (revenue minus cost). The format is what gets handed to the chef and what defends the menu in panel. 5/6 students during this period: review their cost model with guide support.

Panel Day 1. Five teams present + defend (~15 min each, including Q&A). Restaurant partners rotate as panel members for teams other than their own. Blind taste test runs in parallel — menus identified by number only.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

G8 slope review session 2: multi-day scaling (8.EE.B.5). Students take the five-day cost-per-meal series from their cost model and treat it as a linear relationship — does cost grow linearly across days, or are there step changes (weekend price differences, ingredient lot effects)? If the relationship is linear, they extract a slope. If it's not, they articulate why and what graph type fits better. The transferability move: slope is a frame to apply, not a formula to execute, and not every dataset wants it.

blocks.ttl: Cost model: verify each figure traces to source
blocks.desc: L22 split (2 of 2). Check every figure in the cost model against its source. If a number can't be traced, it gets fixed or removed. The traceability check is the actual assessment of whether the team did the underlying work themselves.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

blocks.ttl: Cost model build: ingredients + labor + overhead
blocks.desc: L22 split (1 of 2). Assemble ingredients + labor + overhead into one cost-per-student-per-meal figure for each of the five days. Spreadsheet-builds happen here. Every figure must trace back to a real source (recipe card, supplier call, chef input).

G7 markup and margin (7.RP.A.3). Lesson 19 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 only. Students apply the cost-plus-margin pricing convention to derive what the restaurant would theoretically charge a retail customer versus what it costs Forge. Worked example: if wholesale ingredient cost is $X and margin convention is 30%, retail price is $X / (1-0.30). The point is not the formula — it's understanding why restaurants run on margin percentages and what that means for their pricing logic.

L45. Guides give direct, honest feedback on every draft. Does the nutritional argument hold? Can every member defend their section? Are charts legible and labeled? Is the cost model traceable to real sources? Teams revise immediately after.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
L31 split (2 of 3). Continue nutritional analysis through the rest of the 5-day menu. Macronutrient totals per day across the week. Goal: data ready for gap analysis next session.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

G8 framing of retail vs wholesale as a proportionality problem (7.RP.A.2 — note: this block is on the G8 cohort using a G7 standard for review). Students treat the retail and wholesale price-per-unit as two constants of proportionality and compute total cost for their ingredient list under each. The G8 review move: this is the same machinery as last year, just with two constants instead of one. Lesson 18 from the Challenge spec.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

Sits between gap-analysis (10:25 end) and chef communication (11:00 start) on wk7 d3. Math block surfaced day-nutrient gaps; this block translates each gap into a physiological-consequence sentence the chef pitch will use. 0-5 recap: gap analysis surfaced ~5 day-nutrient gaps; pick top 3. 5-15 modeling: take one gap (e.g., 'Tuesday is 12g short on protein') → write physiological consequence ('slower amino-acid pool refill → weaker repair signal post-lunch'). 15-30 team work: 1-sentence physiological-consequence statement for each of their 3 gaps. 30-35 stress-test: pick strongest sentence to feed into chef communication block. New context for MS-LS1-7 — applies cellular respiration knowledge to their own data; output is load-bearing for chef block.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.
Added (5/6). Substituted for cross-system conversion. Builds fluency on fraction multiplication + same-system unit conversion before scaling. Confirms readiness to do the real recipe scaling.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L4. Macronutrients — what carbs, proteins, fats each do in the body. Not interchangeable. 5/6: carbs as fuel, protein as building material, fat as backup fuel. 7/8: same plus glycogen storage, amino-acid chains, lipid structure.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

blocks.ttl: Ratio: unit rate as cost per ounce
blocks.desc: L11/G6. Ratio as unit rate (cost per ounce). Bridge from a:b to per-unit thinking. Sets up the cost-per-meal calculation later.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

blocks.ttl: FULL DAY: Kitchen visit (cook in partner kitchen)
blocks.desc: External event. Full-day visit to the partner's kitchen. Teams cook the finalized menu items under chef supervision — they prep, measure, execute. Not observation. Also shoot social-media footage where the partner permits.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
L11+L12 combined (I do). G6 'I do' / model phase. Guide works one ratio + fraction-mult problem from a real recipe card start to finish on the board. Students follow along, do not work yet.
L31 split (3 of 3). Lock in macronutrient totals for the whole 5-day menu. Compare to the targets the team set in week 1. Any discrepancies feed directly into gap analysis.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L15 + L16 combined. Scale the full recipe to 30 servings AND produce the shopping list from the scaled card in one continuous block. 5/6: same-system quantities only — guides pre-convert metric. 7/8: full conversions (incl. cross-system) by students. Output: a priced-ready shopping list for the grocery store visit.

G7 cross-system unit conversion: grams ↔ ounces (7.RP.A.1). Lesson 14 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 only. Students apply proportional reasoning across measurement systems — 28.35 g ≈ 1 oz framed as a unit-rate problem, not a memorized conversion. Real ingredient quantities from their recipe card, not abstract numbers. This is prerequisite for the grocery-store visit next week (recipe in metric, store sells in pounds), so accuracy matters more than speed.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L5. Glucose, energy, and cognitive performance. Simple-carb spike-and-crash vs sustained energy from complex carbs. 5/6: ground in their lived experience (candy → great → terrible) then the biology. 7/8: add insulin's role and glycemic index.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

blocks.ttl: Guide review of draft briefs (live feedback)
blocks.desc: Added. Guide reads every team's draft brief, gives direct feedback. Are nutritional targets realistic and traceable to the FDC work? Are the chef questions specific enough to get useful answers? Teams revise after.

G8 framing of recipe scaling as a proportional relationship (8.EE.B.5). Students treat 'servings → ingredient quantity' as a linear function through the origin and compute the constant of proportionality (= the per-serving rate). Same arithmetic as the G7 lesson, different vocabulary — students explicitly write y = kx for one ingredient before scaling. This sets up the cost-function lesson tomorrow where the relationship is no longer through the origin. Lesson 15 from spec, 7/8 track with G8 algebraic framing.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L6. Gut-brain axis and specific nutrient–system connections: serotonin in the gut, iron and oxygen transport, omega-3s and adolescent brain dev, protein → neurotransmitters. Why 'calories' obscures all of this. 5/6: the connections they can feel (nervous → stomach). 7/8: name the neurotransmitters and their precursors.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L5. Glucose, energy, and cognitive performance. Simple-carb spike-and-crash vs sustained energy from complex carbs. 5/6: ground in their lived experience (candy → great → terrible) then the biology. 7/8: add insulin's role and glycemic index.
L37 (combined with menu finalization). Send the gap-analysis follow-up to the chef. Get responses. Lock the final 5-day menu. After this block, no more menu changes.

L38 split (2 of 3). Knife skills: grip, basic cuts, safety. Practice in school with whatever blades are available before the kitchen visit. Students should not pick up a knife for the first time in a professional kitchen.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

G7 concept lesson on unit rate with fractions (7.RP.A.1) — the ratio lesson from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track. If 1/2 person eats 4oz, what does 1 person eat? If a recipe uses 3/4 cup oil per 6 servings, what's the rate per serving? Concept-first: students reason through the relationship before any formal a:b notation or division shortcut. Direct instruction follows for any students who didn't form the concept on their own. Prereq for unit-rate practice (next block) and recipe scaling next week.

G8 interpretation of the two-line wholesale-vs-retail graph (8.EE.B.5). Building on yesterday's plotting block: students articulate what the slope difference means in dollar-per-unit-purchased terms, and where the two lines diverge most across the ingredient list. The analytic question: 'For which ingredient does buying wholesale save the most per unit, and why?' Sets up the wk5 supplier visit — students arrive with hypotheses about which ingredients should benefit most from wholesale.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L45. Guides give direct, honest feedback on every draft. Does the nutritional argument hold? Can every member defend their section? Are charts legible and labeled? Is the cost model traceable to real sources? Teams revise immediately after.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Linear function preview: rate of change
blocks.desc: G8 preview lesson on linear functions as rate-of-change (8.F.B.4), priming the slope work that anchors all G8 math through the Challenge. Tables of (x, y) pairs from real contexts (price per unit, time per task), students compute Δy/Δx and articulate what it represents. No formal y=mx+b yet — that arrives in wk4 d1. The point of this lesson is the language: 'rate of change' and 'unit rate' name the same idea, which is the bridge from G7 proportional reasoning into G8 algebra.
L27 part. What a client brief is and why it exists. Guide models a complete brief using a fictional restaurant. Sets up the team-draft block that follows immediately.

G7 multi-step rational-number operations applied to a recipe (7.EE.B.3). Students scale their recipe card from 1 serving to 30 in a single chained calculation per ingredient — fraction multiplication, decimal arithmetic, and same-system unit conversion all in one expression. The point is fluency at composing operations, not learning new ones. Mistakes here surface as 'where in the chain did you actually go wrong' — guides should push students to identify the failure step rather than re-do the whole problem.

L33 (raw). Run all four chemical tests on raw ingredient samples from the team's menu. Record results, draft interpretive statements about what each test confirms and what it can't. Goes into the presentation record.

G7 recipe scaling complete with shopping-list output (7.RP.A.2, 7.EE.B.3). Students consolidate the unit-rate, fraction-mult, and cross-system conversion work from wk3-4 into a single artifact: the scaled-to-30-servings ingredient list with every quantity in store-purchasable units. Lesson 16 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track. This shopping list is the input to Tuesday's grocery store visit — every line that's not store-ready is a line a student can't price.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L25. Bar chart: macronutrient totals per day vs the team's agreed targets across the 5-day menu. Shows where the menu hits and where it falls short — drives the gap analysis lesson.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L41. Edit kitchen-visit footage into a 60–90 second video for the partner's social media. Content serves the restaurant, not the student. Clean cuts and audible audio first; transitions and color come if there's time.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Finalize the shopping list before the grocery visit. Confirm units match what stores actually sell in. Identify every conversion still needed at the store. No store visit until this list is clean.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

blocks.ttl: Client brief: model + team draft (I do / We do)
blocks.desc: L27 + L28 combined. Each team drafts the brief for their actual restaurant partner: $10/meal ceiling, nutritional targets, dietary constraints, serving size 30, three specific questions for the chef. Drafts get reviewed before the meeting — this block produces the draft, not the final.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

G8 review of slope as rate-of-change (8.EE.B.5). Students who have seen slope before rebuild it: rise over run, slope as 'how much y changes per unit x.' Quick concept check on simple linear graphs before the practice block runs after lunch. The deliberate framing here is 'slope = unit rate at scale' — explicitly tying slope to the proportional reasoning the cohort just did, so cost-per-meal and labor-per-hour later land as slope problems, not separate machinery.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L46. Every team member presents their section and answers simulated panel questions. Guides play the panel and ask hard questions. Any student who cannot answer goes back and learns the material before the real panel.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: FULL DAY: Panel Day 2 + mastery assessments + final reflection
blocks.desc: Panel Day 2 + individual mastery assessments + final reflection. Remaining five teams present. Then every student does a 10-min individual conversation with a guide on their menu's nutritional rationale, scaling math, and cost model — the no-passenger rule is enforced here. End with reflection + announcement of which menus enter the Forge lunch rotation.

blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 1 on food and the body
blocks.desc: L36. Seminar on a short reading about food and the body. Reading assigned the day before with 2–3 questions due on arrival. 15 min discussion max. Reinforces what's already been taught — does not introduce new content.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Gap analysis: which day, which nutrient
blocks.desc: L32. Where the menu fails to hit nutritional targets — which day, which nutrient. Students identify the gap and draft a specific request back to the chef. 5/6: guide helps translate analysis → polite request. 7/8: students draft the follow-up communication, guide reviews before sending.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L24 application. Bar chart of cost-per-student-per-meal across the 5 days, with the $10 ceiling marked. Student-built using the chart skills from the construction lesson.

L5. Glucose, energy, and cognitive performance. Simple-carb spike-and-crash vs sustained energy from complex carbs. 5/6: ground in their lived experience (candy → great → terrible) then the biology. 7/8: add insulin's role and glycemic index.

L21 split (1 of 2). Overhead = packaging + delivery + restaurant margin. Worked example. 5/6 takes overhead as a flat dollar add-on; 7/8 derives it as a percentage of ingredient cost (how restaurants actually compute it).
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Week 1. Teams and roles. Mixed-age team assignments are deliberate — older students are expected to teach younger ones throughout the challenge. The no-passenger rule is named and explained here.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L30. Restaurant meeting debrief. What did you learn, what surprised you, what do you still need to find out, what math do you need to do now you have the actual recipe card. This lesson surfaces gaps before scaling work begins.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

External event. Full-day grocery store visit. Teams take their finalized shopping lists and price every ingredient at retail — price, package size, unit, total cost for the quantity their scaled recipe needs. The unit-conversion work from earlier weeks has to be solid or this visit fails.
L20 split (2 of 2) (G6). Same labor calculation expressed as unit rate ($/meal as a rate). Connects to the unit-rate framing the team has been using throughout pricing.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L40 + L42 combined. Study the restaurant partner's existing online voice (Instagram, website). Write a brief style description. Then write captions for the team's static images and video in that voice. Caption must sound like the restaurant wrote it, not a student.

G7/G8 derivation of overhead as a percentage of ingredient cost (7.RP.A.3). Lesson 21 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track. While 5/6 students are given overhead as a flat dollar figure, 7/8 students derive it the way restaurants actually calculate it: industry-standard overhead percentage applied to ingredient subtotal. Worked example with the guide first, then students apply to their own ingredient subtotal. Output is one number per day that flows into tomorrow's cost-model assembly.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate

External event. Wholesale supplier comes in (or team calls). Students ask specific questions about their own ingredient lists and record answers. Phone-etiquette lesson runs before this so they're ready.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L38 split (1 of 3). Mise en place: what it is, why pro kitchens run on it. How prep gets staged before any heat goes on. Set the cultural expectation before the visit, not at the visit.

G8 slope practice on abstract data (8.EE.B.5). Tables and coordinate graphs without context labels — students extract slope and y-intercept and articulate what each tells them. Deliberately context-free here: the next several lessons drop slope into recipe, retail/wholesale, and labor contexts, and students need the procedural fluency clean before the contexts pile on. Pass bar: produce slope from any two-point table, any line on a grid, in under 60 seconds.

L31 split (2 of 3). Continue nutritional analysis through the rest of the 5-day menu. Macronutrient totals per day across the week. Goal: data ready for gap analysis next session. G7/G8 mirror of nutrition-2 to satisfy nutrition-macros-final-78's prereq.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate

L43 + L44 combined. Presentation structure (sections, owners, evidence flow, what panel will ask) followed by team draft assembly in the same block. Section assignments are guide-decided — no student self-selects. Younger students must own a substantive section.

Primer for next week's nutritional analysis. G7/G8 don't have the bar chart block, so this block teaches the macro-target framework directly using industry-standard daily macro % targets. 0-5 hook: 'Tomorrow you start nutritional analysis — what does your body actually need each day?' 5-15 mini-teach: protein → tissue repair, carb → ATP demand, fat → energy storage + cell membranes. 15-30 stations (3 × 5min): predict the physiological consequence of under-hitting each macro for one day. 30-35 share worst predicted consequence. New context for MS-LS1-7 — primes the wk7 nutritional analysis with biology rationale.
L27 part. What a client brief is and why it exists. Guide models a complete brief using a fictional restaurant. Sets up the team-draft block that follows immediately.

G7 same-system unit conversion in proportional form (7.RP.A.2). Tablespoons↔teaspoons, cups↔fluid ounces, etc., framed explicitly as proportions (3 tsp = 1 tbsp → x tsp / y tbsp). Students convert quantities from their actual scaled recipe card. The G7 framing is 'proportional reasoning,' not 'memorize conversion factors' — when they hit cross-system conversions tomorrow, the proportional framing is what carries over. Lesson 13 from spec, 7/8 track.

L9 split (2 of 2). What each nutritional field means and how to read it. Students look up three foods they actually eat and write a paragraph each on what the data says. Bring results to the next session.

G8 labor cost as the slope of a linear cost function (8.EE.B.5). Lesson 20 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track with G8 algebraic framing. Students model labor as $/hour (= slope) × hours = total labor cost; the y-intercept is zero (labor only exists when prep happens). Same arithmetic as G7's proportional version, different mathematical object. The output feeds into the wk6 cost-model assembly where labor becomes one of three slope terms in the full cost function.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

G7/G8 cognitive performance data review with scatter plots (8.SP.A.1). Lesson 35 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 framing. Students plot their daily cognitive scores against lunch composition (e.g., grams of protein, glycemic index of carbs) and look for patterns. Concept introduced: positive/negative association, no association, outliers. Guides ask questions only — students name the patterns themselves. The follow-up move (per spec): what would a controlled experiment look like, and what does that imply about how much to trust a single data point?

blocks.ttl: Supplier visit debrief + wholesale data entry
blocks.desc: Process the wholesale supplier visit. Enter wholesale pricing into the team's spreadsheet alongside retail. Sets up the retail-vs-wholesale lesson and the cost model.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L11+L12 combined (We do / You do). Apply ratios + unit rate to the full recipe card. Every team scales their actual recipe ingredient by ingredient. G7/G8 cohort.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.
L31 split (3 of 3). Lock in macronutrient totals for the whole 5-day menu. Compare to the targets the team set in week 1. Any discrepancies feed directly into gap analysis.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L4. Macronutrients — what carbs, proteins, fats each do in the body. Not interchangeable. 5/6: carbs as fuel, protein as building material, fat as backup fuel. 7/8: same plus glycogen storage, amino-acid chains, lipid structure.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Added (G6). Standalone mean/median/mode instruction. I do / We do format. Students need this before reading the cognitive data, not during.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
L34. Run the four tests on samples from another team's menu. Cross-checking. Results go into both teams' presentation records — keeps every team accountable to a peer audit before the panel.
Criterion-based fluency gate covering all procedures from Weeks 2 through 6. 11 items, paper and pencil, no calculator, single sitting, 25 minutes testing plus 10 minutes guide scoring.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

G8 two-line coordinate graph: wholesale vs retail (8.EE.B.5). Students plot two linear cost functions on the same axes — wholesale and retail prices for the same ingredient — and read off the slope difference. Lesson 26 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 only. The wholesale price doesn't exist yet (that's wk5's supplier visit), so use representative wholesale figures provided by the guide. The point is to build the interpretive frame before the real data arrives.
L29 split (2 of 2). Role-play the meeting. Every student introduces themselves and asks one of their brief questions out loud before they ever do it for real. Guides play the chef.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Criterion-based fluency gate covering all procedures from Weeks 2 through 6. 11 items, paper and pencil, no calculator, single sitting, 25 minutes testing plus 10 minutes guide scoring.
Revise the brief based on guide review. Brief must be clean, specific, and answer-ready before the restaurant meeting. No team meets a partner with an unreviewed brief.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L10 (combined). Set up the daily cognitive routine and run it once to confirm students know the procedure cold. Why we test, what each test measures (reaction time / Stroop / digit span), how the shared Sheet works, how lunch logging fits in — followed immediately by the first live 6-minute run. After this session, the routine runs every day at 12:45. (Folded together from former L10 split: setup + first-run.)
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

G7 application of unit rate to recipe (7.RP.A.1, 7.RP.A.2). Students take their recipe card from the wk3 Restaurant meeting prep work and compute per-serving rates for each ingredient. This is the I-do/We-do entry point for recipe scaling: the guide models on one ingredient, then students run their own card. Output is a per-serving rate column added to the recipe card — sets up tomorrow's multi-step scaling. Lesson 11/12 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

Anchored to the bar chart of macronutrient targets built in wk6 d2. Lesson asks WHY the body needs each target. 0-5 pull up yesterday's chart. 5-15 mini-teach: protein → tissue repair (growth side of MS-LS1-7), carb → ATP demand, fat → energy storage + cell membranes. 15-30 stations (3 × 5min): kids predict the physiological consequence of under-hitting each macro for one day. 30-35 share worst predicted consequence. New context for MS-LS1-7 — kids interrogate their own bar chart with biology.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
L21 split (2 of 2). Apply overhead to the team's own menu. Each day in the five-day menu gets an overhead line. Output: a complete cost line ready to assemble in cost-model build.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L38 split (2 of 3). Knife skills: grip, basic cuts, safety. Practice in school with whatever blades are available before the kitchen visit. Students should not pick up a knife for the first time in a professional kitchen.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

G7/G8 cost model build: full assembly (7.EE.B.3, 7.RP.A.3). Lesson 22 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 track. Students assemble ingredients + labor + overhead into a single cost-per-student-per-meal figure for each of the five days, with every line traceable to a real source (grocery receipt, supplier quote, chef prep-time estimate). Multi-step rational-number operations all in one place. The check that matters: is every figure defensible? A model with an unsourced number does not clear the bar.
Added (5/6). Mean/median/mode applied to the actual cognitive data the class has been gathering. Pulls the statistics out of the abstract and into something they care about (their own scores).
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

G7 framing of retail vs wholesale as a slope-comparison problem (8.EE.B.5 — note: this block is on the G7 cohort but uses 8.EE.B.5 as the analytic frame to preview next-year's content). Students plot retail price-per-unit and wholesale price-per-unit as two lines and compute the slope of each. The pedagogical play: G7 students who own this framing now have a head start when they encounter slope formally next year. Lesson 18 from the Challenge spec.

blocks.ttl: Slope review: what if cost isn't fixed?
blocks.desc: G8 slope review with a moving y-intercept (8.EE.B.5). Quick re-teach: students have been treating fixed costs (labor, overhead) as constant. What if they're not? Walk through the case where a chef's hourly rate increases at scale, or overhead-per-meal drops as volume rises. The graph: slope changes, or y-intercept shifts. The point is to keep slope conceptually robust before the wk7 nutritional/gap analysis pulls it back into a different context (cost-per-nutrient).
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

G8 verifies the function-form representation of recipe scaling (8.F.B.4). Students take their fully scaled ingredient list and confirm each line obeys y = kx (where k is the per-serving rate) — pick three ingredients, compute (rate × 30), match against their scaled quantity, reconcile any discrepancy. The check surfaces arithmetic errors before they get baked into the shopping list and propagate to the cost model. Sets up the cost-function work in wk6 where the relationships stop being through-origin.

blocks.ttl: Guide review of draft briefs (live feedback)
blocks.desc: Added. Guide reads every team's draft brief, gives direct feedback. Are nutritional targets realistic and traceable to the FDC work? Are the chef questions specific enough to get useful answers? Teams revise after.

L33 (raw). Run all four chemical tests on raw ingredient samples from the team's menu. Record results, draft interpretive statements about what each test confirms and what it can't. Goes into the presentation record.
L34. Run the four tests on samples from another team's menu. Cross-checking. Results go into both teams' presentation records — keeps every team accountable to a peer audit before the panel.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

blocks.ttl: Decimal fluency: addition/subtraction on ingredient prices
blocks.desc: Decimal addition/subtraction on real ingredient prices. Fluency block — students need this solid before retail-pricing calculations land. Pull dollar figures from the actual grocery shopping list.
L12. Independent practice on fraction × whole number using recipe quantities. Goal: fluency before scaling work begins next week.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L38 split (3 of 3) (anchored). Safety + movement in a working kitchen. How to move without creating hazards, cleaning as you go, when to ask the chef vs improvise. Locked in before the kitchen visit.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

G7 cross-system unit conversion session 2: tablespoons → milliliters (7.RP.A.1). Continuation of yesterday's grams↔ounces work. Same proportional framing, different system pair. Students complete the cross-system conversions remaining on their recipe card. Output: a fully numeric, store-ready ingredient list with every quantity in a unit the grocery store actually sells. Lesson 14 from spec, 7/8 only. Hard prereq for next week's grocery visit.
Practice Q&A on the cost model. Teams pitch their cost model to another team and answer hostile questions. Catches weak spots before guide review and panel.

blocks.ttl: Chem tests: demo + practice (iodine / Benedict's / brown paper / Biuret)
blocks.desc: L7 + L8 combined into one 90-minute block (formerly 4 separate demo + practice sessions). Guide demo first on contrasting samples (whole foods vs processed) so the chemistry is concrete: iodine for starch (blue-black indicates starch), Benedict's for simple sugars (blue → orange → brick red with rising concentration when heated), brown paper spot test for fats (translucency under light), Biuret for protein (blue → purple). Students then run all four tests on a new sample set, recording results and writing interpretive statements. 5/6: simple statements ('starch present; can't tell how much'). 7/8: note degree of color change and what that suggests about relative concentration. Name the limit explicitly at the end: tests confirm presence and rough quantity, not grams. By end of session every student should be able to run all four tests independently.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L9 split (1 of 2). USDA FoodData Central: how to navigate the database, find a whole food vs a packaged item, locate specific nutritional fields. Pure mechanics — students should be able to search and pull up a record fluently before interpretation comes in.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Continued team-draft work on the full presentation. Charts, evidence, defended claims. Every section traceable back to the source data the team gathered.
Continued team-draft work on the full presentation. Charts, evidence, defended claims. Every section traceable back to the source data the team gathered.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L12 application (G5). Apply fraction multiplication to the team's full recipe card, ingredient by ingredient. Same-system quantities only — guides pre-convert any metric quantities.

L29 split (1 of 2). What a professional client meeting looks like. Introductions, what you need to learn before you leave, what to do when you don't understand the chef, how to take useful notes during the meeting.
Final-pass revisions on the deck and the speaking parts based on guide-review feedback. Last chance to tighten before the simulated panel.

blocks.ttl: Supplier visit debrief + wholesale data entry
blocks.desc: Process the wholesale supplier visit. Enter wholesale pricing into the team's spreadsheet alongside retail. Sets up the retail-vs-wholesale lesson and the cost model.
L12. Independent practice on fraction × whole number using recipe quantities. Goal: fluency before scaling work begins next week.
L37 (combined with menu finalization). Send the gap-analysis follow-up to the chef. Get responses. Lock the final 5-day menu. After this block, no more menu changes.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 2 on calories as limited metric
blocks.desc: L36. Seminar on a short reading about why calories alone is a poor metric for nutrition. Same format as Reading 1. 5/6 gets a simpler/adapted version of both readings.

L31 split (1 of 3). First pass at nutritional analysis. Students enter day-1 menu items into FDC and pull macronutrient data per serving. 5/6: 3 macros + 2-3 key micros (iron, calcium). 7/8: full macros + 6-8 micros.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Week 1. Teams and roles. Mixed-age team assignments are deliberate — older students are expected to teach younger ones throughout the challenge. The no-passenger rule is named and explained here.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

Added (Week 6). How to make a professional call: how to introduce yourself, how to ask about pricing, how to record what you learn, how to handle a 'we'll call you back'. Practice on each other before any real supplier call.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L11. Independent practice on ratio reasoning before fraction-mult formalization. Use real recipe quantities, not abstract numbers.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
L29 split (2 of 2). Role-play the meeting. Every student introduces themselves and asks one of their brief questions out loud before they ever do it for real. Guides play the chef.

L46. Every team member presents their section and answers simulated panel questions. Guides play the panel and ask hard questions. Any student who cannot answer goes back and learns the material before the real panel.

blocks.ttl: Proportionality table: wholesale + retail as two lines
blocks.desc: G7/G8 proportionality table: wholesale + retail as two lines (8.EE.B.5). Lesson 26 from the Challenge spec, 7/8 only. Students populate a side-by-side table for every ingredient in their menu — wholesale slope vs retail slope — and produce the two-line graph that goes into the panel presentation. This is the data-visualization bookend to the wk4 plotting work, now with real wholesale numbers from the wk5 supplier visit. 5/6 students at this time begin nutritional analysis from wk7.
L13 split (1 of 2). Unit conversion within volume — teaspoon → tablespoon → cup → pint → quart. Use real recipe-card quantities. Same-system only. 7/8 reviews quickly and moves toward cross-system.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Added (5/6). Mean/median/mode applied to the actual cognitive data the class has been gathering. Pulls the statistics out of the abstract and into something they care about (their own scores).

L11. Introduce ratios before formal notation. 'If one person needs 4oz, how much do 30 need?' Reason through it intuitively first. 5/6: stay with whole-number examples. 7/8: move to formal notation (a:b, ratio-as-fraction) faster.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Criterion-based fluency gate covering all procedures from Weeks 2 through 6. 11 items, paper and pencil, no calculator, single sitting, 25 minutes testing plus 10 minutes guide scoring.
L20 split (2 of 2) (G5). Apply hourly rate × prep time / 30 to produce labor cost per student per meal. Simple arithmetic on numbers students already gathered.
L18 (G6). Same content as G5's retail vs wholesale block, but expressed as percentage difference / unit rate. Sets up the markup conversation for 7/8.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

blocks.ttl: Gap analysis: which day, which nutrient
blocks.desc: L32. Where the menu fails to hit nutritional targets — which day, which nutrient. Students identify the gap and draft a specific request back to the chef. 5/6: guide helps translate analysis → polite request. 7/8: students draft the follow-up communication, guide reviews before sending.
Final-pass revisions on the deck and the speaking parts based on guide-review feedback. Last chance to tighten before the simulated panel.

L33 (cooked). Same tests on cooked samples — what changes when an ingredient is cooked? Compare to the raw results from earlier in the challenge. Strengthens the team's claim about what the menu actually delivers nutritionally.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L31 split (1 of 3). First pass at nutritional analysis. Students enter day-1 menu items into FDC and pull macronutrient data per serving. 5/6: 3 macros + 2-3 key micros (iron, calcium). 7/8: full macros + 6-8 micros.
Finalize the shopping list before the grocery visit. Confirm units match what stores actually sell in. Identify every conversion still needed at the store. No store visit until this list is clean.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L38 split (3 of 3) (anchored). Safety + movement in a working kitchen. How to move without creating hazards, cleaning as you go, when to ask the chef vs improvise. Locked in before the kitchen visit.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
L38 split (1 of 3). Mise en place: what it is, why pro kitchens run on it. How prep gets staged before any heat goes on. Set the cultural expectation before the visit, not at the visit.

L6. Gut-brain axis and specific nutrient–system connections: serotonin in the gut, iron and oxygen transport, omega-3s and adolescent brain dev, protein → neurotransmitters. Why 'calories' obscures all of this. 5/6: the connections they can feel (nervous → stomach). 7/8: name the neurotransmitters and their precursors.
